perturbation.original_text: Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall not include on-site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.changed_text: Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall also include on-site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall not include" to "shall also include", there is a contradiction within the definition of 'Product Business'. The original definition specifically excludes on-site configuration. The new definition includes it. This impacts Article III, Seller's Agreement, because it now becomes ambiguous whether Seller is allowed to engage in on-site configuration during the Non-Competition Period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (4) "Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall also include on-site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration. (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis, are not Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements. (6) "Service Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed on Exhibits B and C hereto.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically excluded on-site configuration from the definition of 'Product Business', while the changed text includes it. The contradicted text includes the changed definition of 'Product Business'. This creates a direct contradiction within the definition itself, as on-site configuration is both excluded and included. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis, are not Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, are also Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation alters the definition of "Outsourcing and Professional Services". Originally, agreements consisting primarily of lower-end services (break/fix, IMAC etc.) are NOT considered Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements if not priced on a per-seat basis. The modified definition removes the 'other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis' clause, so that all lower-end services agreements, including break/fix, IMAC, etc., are considered "Outsourcing and Professional Services". This contradicts how Article III restricts Seller's activities related to Outsourcing and Professional Services.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, are also Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements. (6) "Service Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed on Exhibits B and C hereto. (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' by removing the exception for lower-end services not priced on a per-seat basis. Originally, such services were explicitly excluded. Now, according to the changed text, all lower-end services are included. The provided 'Contradicted Text' section uses this same altered definition. While it is in the same location, this does not mean it is not a contradiction, as the original definition was altered to include all lower-end services, creating an in-text contradiction as this new definition will apply to all parts of the agreement. The original exclusion no longer exists, creating a direct conflict and a strong contradiction. The explanation given, justifies the change creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in a completely different manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "substantially the same manner" to "completely different manner" introduces a direct contradiction. Article II initially mandates cooperation in delivering services and products similarly to the pre-closing period. However, the change forces the parties to cooperate in delivering Services and Products in completely different manner. This creates uncertainty and potential disputes because it goes against the original intention of ensuring a smooth transition and continuity of service.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in a completely different manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that services and products will be delivered in "substantially the same manner", while the changed text dictates delivering them in a "completely different manner". The contradicted text now contains the contradictory changed text. This creates a direct in-text contradiction because the delivery manner cannot be both substantially the same and completely different at the same time.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is also competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "CompuCom is not competing" to "CompuCom is also competing" introduces a direct conflict. The initial clause in Section 3.1(b) conditions Seller's obligation to encourage customers to procure from CompuCom on CompuCom not competing with Seller. The change means Seller must encourage customers to procure from CompuCom even when CompuCom is competing. This creates ambiguity around the referral obligation and potential disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is also competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods).
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the seller will encourage customers to procure from CompuCom only if CompuCom is NOT competing with the seller. The changed text introduces a direct contradiction because it states that the seller must encourage customers to procure from CompuCom even when CompuCom IS competing with the seller. This directly contradicts the initial condition, creating a strong in-text contradiction that makes the obligation ambiguous and creates potential disputes, adhering to the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify
perturbation.changed_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will reject orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify
perturbation.explanation: Changing "accept orders" to "reject orders" directly contradicts the purpose of Article IV. The article is intended to outline the conditions under which CompuCom *must* accept orders from Seller. The change creates a scenario where CompuCom is obligated to reject orders if the stated conditions are met. This undermines the entire framework of Article IV and makes 3.1b and IV contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will reject orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify <PAGE> Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'accept orders' with 'reject orders' under the same conditions. The contradicted text restates the changed text, emphasizing the rejection of orders when the stated conditions are met. This directly conflicts with the original intent of the section, which was to define conditions under which CompuCom would accept orders. The modification introduces a clear contradiction by stating that CompuCom will reject orders when the original intent was acceptance, creating an inconsistency as per the perturbation definition.
